---
title: "Broadcasting, Reductions, and einsum: The Algebra of Axes"
description: "Broadcasting is the most useful feature in PyTorch and the most dangerous, because it turns a shape mismatch into a silently wrong answer instead of an error. Here's the rule, the bug it enables, and the notation that makes both disappear."
author: "Maitreya Suin"
categories: [fundamentals, tensors]
date: 2026-08-11
depth: "Note"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
---

> **TL;DR** — Broadcasting aligns shapes from the right and stretches size-1 axes for free (stride 0, no copy). That convenience is also a footgun: `(5,)` minus `(5,1)` doesn't error, it gives you a `(5,5)` outer product, and your loss function keeps training on nonsense. Reductions are the inverse operation, `keepdim` exists specifically to feed the result back into a broadcast, and `einsum` is the one notation that makes the whole thing explicit enough that the bug can't happen.

## Why this comes up

"Implement a dot product without `torch.dot`" is the friendliest possible version of a question that is really asking: *do you know that most of deep learning is "multiply along an axis, then sum it away"?* Attention scores, covariance, convolution, every loss function — all the same move. Once you see the pattern you stop memorizing ops.

## Build it: the right-alignment rule

Two tensors are broadcast-compatible if, **aligning their shapes from the right**, each pair of dimensions is either equal, or one of them is 1. Missing leading dimensions count as 1.

```
    a:  (4, 1, 16)
    b:      (8, 16)   ->  treated as (1, 8, 16)
    ------------------
  a+b:  (4, 8, 16)
```

The size-1 axes get **stretched for free** — as we saw in the strides post, "stretching" means setting a stride of 0, so the same element is read repeatedly. No memory is allocated for the expansion itself. (Mind the qualifier: free is the *expansion*. The op still evaluates every logical output element and allocates the **result** at the full broadcast shape — which is the monster waiting in the Break-it section.)

Now the dot product, which is just *elementwise multiply, then reduce*:

```{python}
import torch
torch.manual_seed(0)
a, b = torch.randn(16), torch.randn(16)

candidates = {
    "(a*b).sum()":          (a*b).sum(),
    "(a*b).sum(dim=-1)":    (a*b).sum(dim=-1),
    "a @ b":                a @ b,
    "torch.inner(a, b)":    torch.inner(a, b),
    "einsum('i,i->', a, b)": torch.einsum('i,i->', a, b),
}
ref = torch.dot(a, b)
for name, val in candidates.items():
    print(f"{name:24s} {val.item():+.6f}   matches torch.dot: {torch.allclose(val, ref)}")
```

```
(a*b).sum()              +2.983946   matches torch.dot: True
(a*b).sum(dim=-1)        +2.983946   matches torch.dot: True
a @ b                    +2.983946   matches torch.dot: True
torch.inner(a, b)        +2.983946   matches torch.dot: True
einsum('i,i->', a, b)    +2.983946   matches torch.dot: True
```

All five are right. They are not all equally good answers.

`a @ b` works here **only because both operands are 1-D** — `@` is overloaded by rank (vector·vector → scalar; matrix·vector; matrix·matrix; batched). The moment the interviewer says "now do it for a batch of vectors, `(B, D)`, one scalar per row," `@` needs contortions and the elementwise-plus-reduce form just... generalizes:

```{python}
A, Bt = torch.randn(4, 16), torch.randn(4, 16)
print("(A*Bt).sum(-1)          ", (A*Bt).sum(-1).shape)
print("einsum('bd,bd->b')      ", torch.einsum('bd,bd->b', A, Bt).shape)
print("forced through matmul   ", (A.unsqueeze(1) @ Bt.unsqueeze(2)).squeeze(-1).squeeze(-1).shape, " <- awkward")
```

```
(A*Bt).sum(-1)           torch.Size([4])
einsum('bd,bd->b')       torch.Size([4])
forced through matmul    torch.Size([4])  <- awkward
```

(Note the two *explicit* `squeeze(-1)` calls in the matmul contortion. An earlier draft used bare `.squeeze()` — which removes **every** size-1 axis, so at batch size 1 it collapses `(1, 1, 1)` to a scalar and your batch dimension vanishes. Bare `.squeeze()` in shape-handling code is its own classic bug; if this post is going to mock the matmul route, it should at least mock it correctly.)

The habit worth building: reach for `dim=-1` rather than a hard-coded axis number whenever the operation is conceptually "along the feature dimension." It survives an extra batch axis appearing upstream, which it eventually will.

### `keepdim` exists for one reason that matters

Reductions collapse an axis. `keepdim=True` leaves a size-1 stub behind — and that stub is *precisely* what broadcasting needs to feed the result back in:

```{python}
x = torch.randn(4, 8)
print("x.sum(-1).shape             ", tuple(x.sum(-1).shape))
print("x.sum(-1, keepdim=True).shape", tuple(x.sum(-1, keepdim=True).shape))

centered = x - x.mean(-1, keepdim=True)     # subtract each row's own mean
print("\nrow means after centering ≈ 0?", centered.mean(-1).abs().max().item() < 1e-6)
```

```
x.sum(-1).shape              (4,)
x.sum(-1, keepdim=True).shape (4, 1)

row means after centering ≈ 0? True
```

Every normalization layer you'll ever write is this pattern. Drop the `keepdim` and you get… the next section.

### What the rule cannot check: meaning

Before the big bug, one quieter observation. Right-alignment matches **sizes**. It has no idea what an axis *means* — and when two different axes happen to share a size, broadcasting will cheerfully line up the wrong one and tell you nothing:

```{python}
B, S, D = 2, 4, 4                # sequence length == feature dim, as happens constantly
x = torch.randn(B, S, D)
w = torch.rand(S)                # intended: one weight PER POSITION

casual   = x * w                 # (S,) aligns from the right -> binds to D. per-FEATURE.
intended = x * w[:, None]        # (S,1) -> broadcasts over D. per-position.

print("x * w  == per-feature binding?", torch.allclose(casual, x * w[None, None, :]))
print("x * w  == what was intended?  ", torch.allclose(casual, intended))
```

```
x * w  == per-feature binding? True
x * w  == what was intended?   False
```

Same integer, different axis, zero warnings — the bug only *exists* because `S == D`, which means it appears when you change a config value and vanishes when you write the unit test with `S=4, D=8`. Two defenses: comment your shapes with meanings (`# (B, S, D)`) so a reader can check intent against alignment, and prefer notations where the meaning is *executable* — which is the entire pitch of the `einsum` section below, where the axis names are the code.

## Break it: the outer product that pretends to be a loss

This is the single most common silent bug in PyTorch, and every person reading this has shipped it at least once.

```{python}
import torch.nn.functional as F
torch.manual_seed(0)
N = 5
pred   = torch.randn(N)       # (5,)   model output, .squeeze()'d somewhere upstream
target = torch.randn(N, 1)    # (5,1)  labels, straight from a DataFrame column

print("pred", tuple(pred.shape), "- target", tuple(target.shape),
      "->", tuple((pred - target).shape), "  <-- (5,5). An OUTER PRODUCT.")

loss_bug = ((pred - target) ** 2).mean()
loss_ok  = ((pred - target.squeeze()) ** 2).mean()
print(f"\nbuggy   MSE = {loss_bug.item():.4f}   (mean over 25 pairwise differences)")
print(f"correct MSE = {loss_ok.item():.4f}   (mean over 5 element-wise differences)")
```

```
pred (5,) - target (5, 1) -> (5, 5)   <-- (5,5). An OUTER PRODUCT.

buggy   MSE = 2.6050   (mean over 25 pairwise differences)
correct MSE = 2.1766   (mean over 5 element-wise differences)
```

No exception. No warning. A scalar loss comes out. It's differentiable. It goes **down** during training — you are, after all, minimizing *something* — and every sample is being penalized against every other sample's label. The model learns to predict the batch mean and you spend a day wondering why your R² is terrible.

The tell is that the loss doesn't scale the way you expect when you change the batch size, but by the time you notice that you've lost an afternoon.

Two mitigations, in order of reliability:

```{python}
#| warning: true
# 1. PyTorch's own loss functions DO warn about this. Use them.
F.mse_loss(pred, target)
```

```
UserWarning: Using a target size (torch.Size([5, 1])) that is different to the
input size (torch.Size([5])). This will likely lead to incorrect results due to
broadcasting. Please ensure they have the same size.
tensor(2.6050)
```

Note carefully what happened: it **warned and then returned a value anyway** — the buggy value. It did not raise. (I originally wrote this cell expecting an error; running it corrected me, which is the whole argument for executing your own claims.)

A warning, not an error — so it will scroll past in a training log. Which is why the second mitigation is the one that actually saves you:

```{python}
def mse(pred, target):
    assert pred.shape == target.shape, f"shape mismatch: {pred.shape} vs {target.shape}"
    return ((pred - target) ** 2).mean()
```

One line. Put it in every loss function you write. Broadcasting is a feature you want *between* a tensor and a statistic of that tensor; between a prediction and its label you almost never want it — the honorable exceptions being a deliberate scalar target (`((pred - 0.0)**2)` is fine) and per-sample weights, which broadcast against the *residual*, not the label. If a loss is genuinely meant to broadcast, write that into its signature and its docstring; what you must never do is let right-alignment make the decision for you.

### The other break: broadcasting can allocate a monster

Broadcasting the *shape* is free. Materializing the *result* is not. The naive pairwise-distance one-liner is the classic:

```{python}
B, Nq, Nk, D = 32, 1024, 1024, 128
elems = B * Nq * Nk * D
print(f"(B,Nq,1,D) - (B,1,Nk,D)  ->  ({B}, {Nq}, {Nk}, {D})")
print(f"= {elems:,} elements = {elems*4/2**30:.1f} GiB in fp32")
print(f"...from two input tensors of {B*Nq*D*4/2**20:.0f} MiB each.")
```

```
(B,Nq,1,D) - (B,1,Nk,D)  ->  (32, 1024, 1024, 128)
= 4,294,967,296 elements = 16.0 GiB in fp32
...from two input tensors of 16 MiB each.
```

Two 16 MiB tensors, one subtraction, 16 GiB of intermediate. That's your OOM. The fix — expanding `‖a−b‖² = ‖a‖² + ‖b‖² − 2a·b` so you never form the `D` axis — is the subject of the numerical-stability post, where it turns out to have a *second*, nastier problem.

## einsum: say what you mean

`einsum` collapses this entire post into one notation. The rule has three clauses, and the third one is the one every short summary (including this post's first draft) gets wrong: an index shared **across operands** aligns those axes for multiplication; an index **absent from the output** is summed away; and an index repeated **within a single operand** walks the diagonal — it selects, it doesn't contract. That's why `ii->i` below is `diagonal(M)` and `ii->` is the trace, not some doubly-summed object.

```{python}
q = torch.randn(2, 4, 6, 8)   # (B, H, S, Dh)
k = torch.randn(2, 4, 6, 8)
A = torch.randn(3, 4, 5); Bm = torch.randn(3, 5, 6)
M = torch.randn(4, 4)

checks = [
    ("attention scores  bhid,bhjd->bhij", torch.einsum('bhid,bhjd->bhij', q, k), q @ k.transpose(-2,-1)),
    ("batched matmul    bij,bjk->bik   ", torch.einsum('bij,bjk->bik', A, Bm),   torch.bmm(A, Bm)),
    ("diagonal          ii->i          ", torch.einsum('ii->i', M),              torch.diagonal(M)),
    ("transpose         ij->ji         ", torch.einsum('ij->ji', M),             M.T),
]
for name, got, want in checks:
    print(f"{name}  == native op: {torch.allclose(got, want, atol=1e-5)}")
```

```
attention scores  bhid,bhjd->bhij  == native op: True
batched matmul    bij,bjk->bik     == native op: True
diagonal          ii->i            == native op: True
transpose         ij->ji           == native op: True
```

Look at the attention line. `bhid,bhjd->bhij` says: *for each batch and head, contract over the head-dim `d`, keeping query position `i` and key position `j`.* That's the definition of the attention score matrix, written as itself. Compare to `q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)`, which is the same computation expressed as "do a transpose you have to mentally verify, then hope the batch dims line up."

**When not to use it:** for a two-operand contraction there is no "order" to optimize — there's one contraction, and it dispatches to the same matmul you'd have written. Contraction-*path* optimization only becomes a real question at **three or more operands** (that's what `opt_einsum` exists for, and where a bad order can genuinely cost you). And in a hot inner loop, a hand-written `matmul` sometimes fuses better under the compiler. But for expressing *intent* — in an interview, in a paper, in code someone else will read — it wins.

## The follow-up

**"`*` vs `@`?"** `*` is elementwise (Hadamard) and broadcasts. `@` is matmul and contracts. Confusing them is the most common beginner error and the fastest thing an interviewer can screen for.

**"How would you compute a covariance matrix?"** `x = x - x.mean(0, keepdim=True); cov = x.T @ x / (n-1)`. It's the same three moves: center via a keepdim-broadcast, contract with `@`, normalize. If you can see that this and attention-scores and pairwise-distance are the same shape of computation, you've got the idea of this post.

**"When is `repeat` correct and `expand` wrong?"** When you're going to **write** to the result. `expand`'s stride-0 axes alias — every "copy" is the same memory cell, so there's no such thing as writing to just one of them, and PyTorch's overlap check will refuse most in-place ops outright (`more than one element of the written-to tensor refers to a single memory location`). Read-only: `expand`. Mutating: `repeat` or `clone`. One practical wrinkle even in the read-only case: some downstream kernels quietly call `contiguous()` on a stride-0 input — if a profile shows an unexplained copy right after an `expand`, that's the usual suspect.

## References

- [PyTorch: Broadcasting semantics](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/broadcasting.html) — the rules, formally.
- [`torch.einsum`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.einsum.html), and Olexa Bilaniuk's [einsum tutorial](https://rockt.github.io/2018/04/30/einsum) for the intuition.
- [`opt_einsum`](https://optimized-einsum.readthedocs.io/) — for when the contraction order actually matters.
- The `(5,) vs (5,1)` bug has its own [PyTorch issue history](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/11959) — you are in extremely good company.
